contexts.chapter_heading: Behavioural and institutional drivers of participation
contexts.source_card_text: AECM can fail either because payments are too low to induce adoption or because they attract baseline-complying farmers with low or zero additionality.
contexts.context_preview: According to economic theory, the failure of PES initiatives can be attributed to inefficiencies that are directly related to participation (Engel et al., 2008; Rolfe et al., 2021).

contexts.chapter_heading: Contract design, flexibility and transaction costs
contexts.source_card_text: For farmers with higher opportunity costs, policy can either increase incentives through differentiated mechanisms or invest in nudging and signalling strategies.
contexts.context_preview: To encourage the participation of farmers with higher opportunity costs, AECM implementers can either ==increase incentives through tailored methods like auctions or differentiated payments== (Rolfe et al., 2021; Schaub et al., 2023), or ==invest in nudging and signalling strategies== (Kuhfuss et al., 2016).

contexts.source_card_text: Environmental attitudes influence whether farmers perceive an AECM as relevant and acceptable.
contexts.context_preview: Farmers’ awareness of the problem being addressed and their level of environmental concern can influence the degree of AECM acceptability and the understanding of the potential environmental benefit of participation (Rogers, 2003; Dessart et al., 2019).

contexts.chapter_heading: Contract design, flexibility and transaction costs
contexts.source_card_text: Farmers generally prefer AECM contracts that are simple, understandable and flexible, while flat payments can deter farmers with high compliance costs.
contexts.context_preview: According to theory, increased AECM complexity, both in terms of intensity of the management practice and increased risk or uncertainty, deters uptake (Rogers, 2003; Pannell et al., 2006).
